# Interpreting the Output Object {#sec-output}

The list returned by `gwzinbr()` contains 22 named elements. This chapter walks
through each one with a practical description and usage examples.

```{r}
#| label: setup-output
#| cache: false
#| echo: false
library(gwzinbr)
```

## Goodness-of-fit: `$measures` and `$bandwidth`

```r
mod$bandwidth   # scalar — bandwidth h used
mod$measures    # named numeric vector
```

`$measures` contains:

| Name | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `deviance` | Residual deviance of the GWR model |
| `AIC` | Akaike Information Criterion |
| `AICc` | Corrected AIC (small-sample adjustment) |
| `pct_ll` | Pseudo-R² (McFadden): $1 - \hat{\ell}/\hat{\ell}_0$ |
| `adj_pct_ll` | Adjusted pseudo-R² |

```r
# Example
mod$measures["AICc"]
mod$measures["pct_ll"]
```

Compare AICc across candidate models (lower is better). Pseudo-R² above 0.2
generally indicates reasonable fit for count models.

## Local coefficient estimates: `$gwr_param_estimates`

```r
mod$gwr_param_estimates   # matrix: N rows × (p + 1) columns
```

Each row corresponds to one observation (spatial unit). Columns are the intercept
followed by each predictor in the formula. These are the **local** estimates
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(\mathbf{u}_i)$.

```r
# Extract the local estimate for the first predictor
beta_x1 <- mod$gwr_param_estimates[, "x1"]

# Add to the data frame for mapping
dados_modelo$beta_x1 <- beta_x1
```

## Local standard errors: `$qntls_gwr_se` and `$descript_stats_gwr_se`

```r
mod$qntls_gwr_se              # quantiles of SE per coefficient
mod$descript_stats_gwr_se     # mean, SD, min, max of SE per coefficient
```

Use these to assess the variability of local standard errors across the study area.
High variability in SE may indicate sparse data in some regions.

## Local summary statistics: `$qntls_gwr_param_estimates` and `$descript_stats_gwr_param_estimates`

```r
mod$qntls_gwr_param_estimates            # quantiles per coefficient
mod$descript_stats_gwr_param_estimates   # mean, SD, min, max per coefficient
```

Quick way to summarise the spatial distribution of each local coefficient:

```r
# Print the full descriptive statistics table
mod$descript_stats_gwr_param_estimates
```

## Significance tests: `$t_test_gwr_param_estimates`

```r
mod$t_test_gwr_param_estimates   # matrix: N rows × (2 × (p + 1)) columns
```

For each observation and each coefficient, two values are stored:
the $t$-statistic and the corresponding two-sided $p$-value.

```r
# Extract p-values for predictor "x1"
pvals_x1 <- mod$t_test_gwr_param_estimates[, "x1_pvalue"]

# Flag significant locations (α = 0.10)
sig_x1 <- pvals_x1 < 0.10
dados_modelo$sig_x1 <- sig_x1
```

Use this to **mask non-significant estimates** in maps (see @sec-visualizacao).

## Overdispersion parameter: `$alpha_estimates`

```r
mod$alpha_estimates   # matrix: N × 2
                      # column 1: local alpha; column 2: location index
```

The local overdispersion parameter $\alpha_i$ quantifies how much the variance
exceeds the mean at each location. Regions with high $\alpha$ have more
unexplained variability.

```r
alpha_local <- mod$alpha_estimates[, 1]
dados_modelo$alpha <- alpha_local
```

This element is `NULL` for Poisson and ZIP models (no overdispersion parameter).

## Residuals: `$residuals`

```r
mod$residuals   # matrix: N × 5
```

The five columns are:

| Column | Residual type |
|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Raw residuals ($y_i - \hat{y}_i$) |
| 2 | Pearson residuals |
| 3 | Deviance residuals |
| 4 | Standardised deviance residuals |
| 5 | Working residuals (from IRLS) |

Deviance residuals (column 3 or 4) are most useful for spatial diagnostics:

```r
resid_deviance <- mod$residuals[, 3]
dados_modelo$residuos <- resid_deviance
```

## Non-stationarity test: `$non_stationary_test`

```r
mod$non_stationary_test   # data frame: one row per coefficient
```

This test assesses whether the spatial variation in each local coefficient is
statistically significant — i.e., whether a globally constant coefficient would
be sufficient. A significant result (small $p$-value) supports retaining the GWR
over a global model for that covariate.

```r
mod$non_stationary_test
#         variable      F_stat   df1   df2   p_value
# (Intercept)          2.341    4.2  91.8     0.032
# x1                   3.112    4.2  91.8     0.004
# x2                   1.067    4.2  91.8     0.390
```

## Global model reference: `$global_param_estimates`

```r
mod$global_param_estimates   # matrix: coefficients and SE of global NB
```

This is the global Negative Binomial fit used as the starting point for the
IRLS algorithm. It serves as a reference for comparing against the GWR local
estimates.

## Zero-inflation elements

The following elements are non-`NULL` only when `model %in% c("zinb", "zip")`:

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `$qntls_gwr_zero_infl_param_estimates` | Quantiles of $\hat{\lambda}(\mathbf{u}_i)$ |
| `$descript_stats_gwr_zero_infl_param_estimates` | Descriptive stats of ZI local estimates |
| `$t_test_gwr_zero_infl_param_estimates` | $t$-tests for zero-inflation coefficients |
| `$qntls_gwr_zero_infl_se` | Quantiles of ZI standard errors |
| `$descript_stats_gwr_zero_infl_se` | Descriptive stats of ZI SE |
| `$non_stationary_test_zero_infl` | Non-stationarity test for ZI component |
| `$analysis_max_like_zero_infl_param_estimated` | MLE ZI parameter estimates |
| `$analysis_max_like_gof_measures` | GoF for global ZI model |

## Prediction grid: `$param_estimates_grid`

```r
mod$param_estimates_grid   # matrix or NULL
```

When a `grid` data frame is passed to `gwzinbr()`, this element contains the
local coefficients predicted at each grid location. Useful for producing smooth
raster maps of spatial variation in coefficients.

## Variance-covariance matrix: `$variance_covariance_matrix`

```r
mod$variance_covariance_matrix   # square matrix: p+1 × p+1
```

The variance-covariance matrix of the global parameter estimates. Can be used to
compute confidence intervals for the global model reference coefficients.
